question: Rollator or walker — which one do I need?

children.text: A walker requires you to lift it and place it forward with each step. A rollator rolls on wheels and has hand brakes. Walkers give more stability for people who need to lean heavily. Rollators are faster, work on smoother surfaces, and usually have a seat for rest breaks. Most people end up on a rollator.

question: How do I size a wheelchair correctly?

children.text: Measure the widest point across the user's hips while seated. Add 1–2 inches for a comfortable seat width. Standard chairs come in 16-, 18-, and 20-inch widths. Too narrow and it causes hip pressure; too wide and the user can't self-propel effectively.

question: What weight capacity do I need on a mobility scooter?

children.text: Add 20% to the user's weight to account for groceries, purse, and real-world use. A 250-lb user needs at least a 300-lb-capacity scooter. Going over the rated capacity damages the motor and voids the warranty.

question: How do I get fitted for a CPAP mask?

children.text: Come in and we'll size you in store. We try 2–3 masks until one seals for your face, sleep position, and breathing style. Mask fit is the #1 predictor of CPAP therapy success — don't skip it.

question: How do I clean my CPAP equipment?

children.text: Warm water and mild dish soap. Daily for the mask cushion. Weekly for the tubing. Monthly for the humidifier chamber. Air dry. Never use bleach. Skip the ozone cleaners — the FDA hasn't approved them for CPAP cleaning.

question: Do I need a prescription for a CPAP?

children.text: Yes — CPAP machines are FDA class II devices and require a current prescription. If you don't have one handy, your sleep specialist or primary care provider can fax it to us. Masks and supplies also generally require a prescription.

question: Can I travel with my CPAP?

children.text: Yes. CPAP machines are allowed in carry-on as medical equipment and don't count toward your carry-on limit. The ResMed AirMini is purpose-built for travel — tiny, waterless humidification, optional battery pack. We stock it alongside the AirSense 11.

question: Can I install a grab bar in my existing bathroom?

children.text: Yes — in almost every bathroom. Grab bars bolt into studs or approved wall anchors for tile/drywall. We sell suction-mount bars too, but we're clear: they're not for load-bearing use. For real fall prevention, bolt into studs. We'll coach you through placement.

question: Will a shower chair fit my tub?

children.text: Most standard tubs are 14–17 inches wide inside. A typical shower chair base is 15–18 inches wide. Measure your tub's interior width and the clear space inside your shower stall. We'll match a bench to your space — or send a transfer bench that straddles the tub.

question: How does the rental deposit work?

children.text: You pay the rental rate plus a refundable deposit at pickup (deposit varies by item — $50 for a knee scooter, $100+ for larger items). When you return the equipment in working condition, we return the deposit the same day.

Yes, within the Portland metro. Free delivery in Lake Oswego. Delivery fees elsewhere depend on distance — typically $25–$75. We also handle pickup at end of rental. Call us or submit a reservation and we'll confirm delivery details.

Some Medicare plans and supplemental coverage reimburse certain DME rentals (hospital beds, wheelchairs). Coverage varies wildly. We don't bill insurance directly for rentals — you'd pay us and seek reimbursement. Ask us and we'll help you determine if your rental is likely to be covered.

children.text: mmHg, explained

Compression is measured in millimeters of mercury. 8-15 mmHg is everyday travel support. 15-20 is mild medical compression for tired legs and mild swelling. 20-30 is standard post-surgical and chronic venous insufficiency. 30-40 is heavier therapeutic compression. Going too tight can actually reduce circulation — we measure and size.
We're a JOBST partner and stock UltraSheer, Opaque, Relief, and FarrowWrap product lines. Bring your prescription if you have one; we'll hold compression levels to whatever your provider ordered.

name: Sleep Apnea
heroHeadline: New CPAP user? We fit you in person.
heroIntro: 90% of CPAP failures are mask fit failures. We spend the time to try multiple masks with you — no rushed handoff.

children.text: Why CPAP prescriptions come to our counter

The moment your sleep specialist hands you a CPAP prescription, you have choices. You can mail-order a machine you've never touched, wait for it to arrive, try it, have it leak, and spend weeks figuring out the problem. Or you can walk into our store, hand us the prescription, and walk out with a machine sized to your face that you've already exhaled into under our supervision.
We carry ResMed AirSense 11 and ResMed AirMini travel machines. Both are gold-standard auto-adjusting CPAP devices.
What to bring to your first fitting

Your current CPAP prescription (a fax or photo from your provider is fine), your insurance information if you're using benefits, and a rough idea of how you sleep — side, back, stomach, mouth-breather, beard, glasses.
The three mask types — and who each one fits
Nasal pillow (smallest)
Sits just inside the nostrils. Best for side sleepers who don't want anything across their face. Requires nose-only breathing.
Nasal mask (middle)
Covers just the nose. The most common starting mask. Good for mild beards.
Full face mask
Covers nose and mouth. Mandatory if you breathe through your mouth while sleeping, or if you have nasal congestion. Heavier and louder, but it's the right choice for a third of CPAP users.
Supply replacement cadence

Most insurance covers CPAP supplies replacement on a schedule: mask cushions every 2 weeks, full masks every 3 months, tubing every 3 months, filters monthly. We track it for you and remind you when you're due.
Cleaning — the question we get asked most

Warm water, mild dish soap, air dry. Daily for the mask cushion, weekly for the tubing, monthly for the machine's water chamber. Never use bleach. The ozone cleaners sold online aren't FDA-approved; we recommend against them.

The typical post-op setup

Depending on what you're recovering from, you'll want some combination of: a rolling walker or knee scooter, a transfer bench or shower chair, a raised toilet seat, a grabber, ice packs, and (for major recovery) a hospital bed and wedge pillow. We have a pre-surgery consultation checklist — call ahead and we'll pull it together.

You reserve by phone or through this site. We confirm availability the same day. Pay the rental fee and a refundable deposit when you pick up. When you're done, call us — we pick up at your home or you drop off at the store. The deposit comes back the same day.
When to pick up

Most surgeons send you home with a list and expect you to have equipment in place before the procedure. Call us 7–10 days ahead so we can reserve the right rentals. Last-minute arrangements are usually possible but inventory is tighter.

name: Mobility Support
heroHeadline: Stay mobile — at home, in the yard, in the world
heroIntro: The right mobility aid is the one that fits your body, your home, and the places you want to go. We'll help you sort it out.

children.text: Start with the question of how much stability

A cane supports about 25% of body weight. A walker supports significantly more and gives bilateral support. A rollator is a walker with wheels — it rolls instead of lifts. A transport chair needs a pusher. A wheelchair is self-propelled. A scooter is electric.
Transitioning between aids
Most people under-use their aid at the start and over-rely on it later. Work with your PT to size to your current function, not your worst day. Re-evaluate every six months.
Home fit matters as much as body fit

Measure your narrowest hallway. Measure your bathroom door. Check if your car trunk holds the folded version. A great rollator that doesn't fit through your bathroom door is worse than a merely-adequate one that does.

name: Caring for an elderly parent
heroHeadline: Caring for a parent at home? Start here.
heroIntro: We've walked hundreds of adult children through the same conversation. Here's the equipment that actually matters.

children.text: Four items that keep someone home instead of in a facility
If your parent is showing unsteady balance, occasional confusion, or has had a small fall, the four pieces of equipment that most reduce risk are:
1. Bolted grab bars
Near the toilet, inside the shower, and at the shower entrance. Suction-mount bars are not acceptable for load-bearing use.
2. Raised toilet seat with arms
Lowers the distance of the sit-and-stand, which is where most bathroom falls happen.
3. Shower chair or transfer bench
Stops the in-shower standing that causes slip-and-fall injuries.
4. Bed handle or rail
Prevents the roll-out-of-bed injury, one of the most common late-night falls.
When you need more than equipment

If your parent has fallen more than once in the past year, can't manage medications, or is losing weight unintentionally, talk with their primary care provider. Equipment reduces risk; it doesn't replace supervision.
Conversations that are hard but necessary
Most adult children wait too long to buy incontinence products. Buy them. Have them on hand. Don't ask permission. Your parent may object at first; they'll adapt fast.

children.text: Absorbency matters more than brand

Skin health is non-negotiable
Pair every product with a barrier cream (zinc-based) and gentle skin cleansers. Skin breakdown happens fast with incontinence and is much harder to reverse than it is to prevent.

children.text: A quad cane bridges the gap between a standard cane and a walker. The four-point base self-stands, so it's there when you reach for it, and offers meaningfully more lateral stability — a common recommendation after a stroke or for one-sided weakness.

name: Drive Two-Button Folding Walker
shortDescription: Classic dual-release folding walker — maximum stability for indoor use, with optional glide caps.

children.text: When stability matters more than speed, a standard walker is still the most supportive option. Two-button release folds it flat for the car. We'll add 5-inch glides or tennis-ball-style caps to the front legs so it slides instead of lifts.

name: Drive Nitro Euro-Style Rollator
shortDescription: Front-angled wheels and a sleek frame that maneuvers tight corners — the rollator customers pick for looks and turning.

children.text: The Nitro's angled front casters turn on a dime, making it the easiest rollator we stock for apartments and narrow hallways. Cross-brace folds it slim enough to stand on its own against a wall.

name: Pride Go-Go Elite Traveller Scooter
shortDescription: Travel power scooter that breaks into five pieces for a car trunk — no lift required.

children.text: The Go-Go is the scooter caregivers can actually load alone. It disassembles into five lightweight pieces, the heaviest around 30 lb, so it fits in a sedan trunk. Great for someone who walks at home but needs range for the store, church, or a parade.

name: Drive Cruiser III Standard Wheelchair
shortDescription: Durable self-propel wheelchair with flip-back desk arms and swing-away footrests for daily home use.

children.text: A full-size self-propelled chair for someone who'll use it daily and has the upper-body strength to push. Flip-back desk arms slide under a table; swing-away footrests make transfers safer.

name: Lumex UpWalker Upright Walker
shortDescription: Stand-tall walker with forearm supports that encourages upright posture and takes weight off the wrists.

children.text: An upright walker lets you walk looking forward instead of hunched over handlebars. Forearm rests shift the load off sore wrists and shoulders, and the higher posture reduces back strain. A favorite for arthritis and spinal stenosis.

name: Drive Bariatric Heavy-Duty Rollator
shortDescription: Reinforced rollator with a wider seat and a 500-lb capacity for heavier users who need a rest seat.

children.text: A wider, reinforced frame rated to 500 lb with a 12-inch padded seat. Built for bariatric users who've outgrown a standard rollator's weight rating but still want the four-wheel-and-seat convenience.

name: ResMed AirFit N20 Nasal Mask
shortDescription: The most-fitted nasal mask — soft InfinitySeal cushion that flexes with movement, for nose-breathers.

children.text: If you breathe through your nose and find pillows irritating, the N20 is usually the answer. Its cushion seals across a wide range of face shapes, which is why it's the nasal mask we reach for first. Comes with multiple cushion sizes to fit in store.

name: ResMed ClimateLineAir Heated Tubing
shortDescription: Heated tubing for the AirSense 11 that prevents rainout — no more cold condensation in the morning.

children.text: If you wake up to water in your tube or mask, heated tubing fixes it. The ClimateLineAir warms the air path so humidity stays vapor instead of condensing. A standard insurance-eligible replacement item.

name: ResMed AirSense 11 Air Filters (6-pack)
shortDescription: Standard disposable filters for the AirSense 11. Swap monthly — a six-pack covers half a year.

children.text: Clean filters keep dust and pollen out of your therapy air and protect the motor. We recommend changing them monthly, or sooner if you have pets. A six-pack is the simplest way to stay ahead of it.

name: ResMed AirSense 11 Humidifier Tub
shortDescription: Replacement water chamber for the AirSense 11 — mineral buildup is normal; swap every 6 months.

children.text: Even with distilled water, humidifier tubs cloud and scale over time. A fresh chamber keeps your humidification consistent and your machine clean. Dishwasher-safe on the top rack between replacements.

name: CPAP Mask Cleaning Wipes (62 ct)
shortDescription: Unscented daily wipes that lift facial oils off the cushion so your mask seals better and lasts longer.

children.text: Skin oil is the enemy of a good mask seal — it breaks down silicone and causes leaks. A quick wipe each morning roughly doubles cushion life. Alcohol-free and unscented so there's nothing to irritate skin overnight.

name: Moen 24" Bathroom Grab Bar
shortDescription: Stainless 24-inch grab bar rated to 500 lb — the single most important bolt-in safety upgrade.

children.text: A properly anchored grab bar is the difference between a stumble and a fall. This 24-inch bar mounts into studs (or with proper anchors) and is rated to 500 lb. We'll show you exactly where to place it for the shower entry or beside the toilet.

name: Drive Tub Transfer Bench
shortDescription: Sit, slide, and swing your legs over the tub wall — the safest way in and out of a bathtub.

children.text: For anyone who can't safely step over a tub wall, a transfer bench is the answer: you sit on the outboard seat, then slide across. Two legs sit outside the tub, two inside. Height-adjustable, with a backrest and a reversible design for either-side tubs.

name: Drive 3-in-1 Bedside Commode
shortDescription: Works three ways: bedside commode, raised toilet seat, or toilet safety frame. A recovery staple.

children.text: One of the most useful single items for recovery at home. Use it as a standalone commode beside the bed at night, drop the pail and place it over the existing toilet as a raised seat with arms, or use the frame alone for stability. Adjustable height.

name: Carex Toilet Safety Rails
shortDescription: Bolt-on arms that give you something sturdy to push up from — installs on most existing toilets.

children.text: If the toilet height is fine but the sit-to-stand is the problem, safety rails add the arms without raising the seat. They attach under the existing seat bolts and adjust for width and height.

name: Handheld Shower Spray with 6-ft Hose
shortDescription: Lets a seated bather rinse without standing or twisting — pairs with any shower chair or transfer bench.

children.text: A handheld sprayer is the quiet hero of a safe shower. Once someone is seated on a bench, a 6-foot hose lets them — or a caregiver — rinse every area without standing up. Tool-free install on a standard shower arm.

name: JOBST UltraSheer 20-30 mmHg Knee-High
shortDescription: Standard therapeutic compression for venous insufficiency and post-surgical swelling — sheer finish.

children.text: 20-30 mmHg is the most commonly prescribed range for chronic venous insufficiency, varicose veins, and post-surgical swelling. The UltraSheer finish keeps it discreet. We measure your ankle, calf, and length to size it correctly — fit is everything at this compression.

name: JOBST Relief 20-30 mmHg Thigh-High
shortDescription: Thigh-high therapeutic compression with a silicone band — for swelling that extends above the knee.

children.text: When swelling reaches the thigh, a knee-high isn't enough. The Relief line is JOBST's value workhorse — durable, with a silicone top band that holds it up without rolling. Fitted in store to your measurements.

name: JOBST for Men 15-20 mmHg Socks
shortDescription: Ribbed dress-sock styling in mild medical compression — looks like a normal sock under slacks.

children.text: Designed to look like a standard ribbed dress sock so men actually wear them. 15-20 mmHg handles tired legs, mild swelling, and long days on your feet or in a plane seat.

name: JOBST Bella Strong Arm Sleeve 20-30 mmHg
shortDescription: Lymphedema arm sleeve for post-mastectomy and chronic swelling — durable knit with a comfort band.

children.text: A common need after breast-cancer surgery and for chronic upper-limb lymphedema. We carry it with and without a gauntlet and fit it to your arm length and circumference. Bring your prescription so we hold the ordered compression.

name: Stocking Donner / Doffer Aid
shortDescription: The tool that makes compression stockings actually wearable when hands or back won't cooperate.

children.text: The number-one reason people abandon compression therapy is they can't get the stockings on. A donner aid solves that — slide the stocking over the frame, then guide your foot and leg in. We'll demo it at the counter.

name: Sterile Gauze Pads 4" x 4" (100 ct)
shortDescription: The workhorse of home wound care — individually wrapped, sterile, 12-ply absorbency.

children.text: Sterile 4x4 gauze is the basis of most home dressing changes. Individually wrapped so each pad stays sterile until you open it. We stock these by the box because nobody ever buys too few.

name: Telfa Non-Stick Pads 3" x 4" (50 ct)
shortDescription: Pads that don't fuse to the wound — so the dressing change doesn't undo the healing.

children.text: Plain gauze can dry into a wound and tear it open on removal. Telfa's perforated film face lifts away cleanly, which is why it's the go-to for surgical incisions and abrasions. Pair with gauze and tape.

name: 3M Micropore Paper Tape 1" (2-pack)
shortDescription: Gentle paper tape that holds a dressing but peels off without tearing fragile skin.

children.text: Older and post-surgical skin is fragile, and aggressive tape causes its own wounds. Micropore holds securely yet releases cleanly — the tape we recommend for daily dressing changes and sensitive skin.

name: Saline Wound Cleanser Spray (7.4 oz)
shortDescription: No-rinse saline spray to flush a wound clean without the sting of harsher antiseptics.

children.text: Gentle, no-rinse, and non-stinging — a controlled saline spray clears debris from a wound bed without damaging healing tissue the way alcohol or peroxide can. The first step in most dressing changes.

name: Hydrocolloid Dressings 4" x 4" (5 ct)
shortDescription: Waterproof, self-adhesive dressings that hold a moist healing environment for pressure sores and blisters.

children.text: Hydrocolloids create the moist, protected environment that speeds healing for early-stage pressure injuries, blisters, and shallow wounds — and they're waterproof, so they stay put for days. Ask us about staging before using these on a pressure sore.

name: Allay Post-Op Wound Care Kit
shortDescription: Everything for two weeks of dressing changes in one box — cleanser, non-stick pads, gauze, and gentle tape.

children.text: We got tired of customers leaving the hospital and hunting for five separate items, so we kit them together. Includes saline cleanser, Telfa non-stick pads, 4x4 gauze, Micropore tape, and gloves — enough for a typical two-week post-op course. Ask for it by name.

name: Depend Pull-On Protective Underwear (Maximum)
shortDescription: Pull-on underwear for active, mobile adults — pulls up and down like regular underwear.

children.text: For someone who's up and about and manages their own toileting, pull-on underwear preserves dignity and independence — it looks and works like regular underwear. We carry men's and women's cuts. Bring a waist measurement and we'll match absorbency.

name: Bladder Control Pads (Heavy, 39 ct)

children.text: For light to moderate leakage, a pad inside regular underwear is all most people need. Adhesive backing keeps it in place, and the profile is thin enough to be invisible under clothing.

name: Tranquility Disposable Underpads 30" x 36" (40 ct)
shortDescription: Heavy-duty bed and chair pads ("chux") that protect the mattress overnight.

children.text: Underpads protect the mattress, chair, or car seat and make cleanup a single step. Tranquility's are more absorbent than the thin hospital versions, with a soft top sheet that's gentler on skin for overnight use.

name: Calmoseptine Skin Barrier Cream (4 oz)
shortDescription: Protects and soothes skin against moisture damage — essential alongside any incontinence routine.

children.text: Prolonged moisture breaks skin down fast. A zinc-based barrier cream protects against incontinence-associated dermatitis and soothes already-irritated skin. A small amount at each change goes a long way.

name: RMS 32" Reacher Grabber
shortDescription: Grab dropped keys, reach a top shelf, or pull on socks — without the bend that ends in a fall.

children.text: A $20 reacher prevents the bend-and-fall and the climb-and-fall — two of the most common ways people end up in the ER. Rotating jaw with a magnetic tip for keys and coins, and enough grip to hold a water bottle.

name: Dressing Aid Kit (sock aid, shoehorn, dressing stick)
shortDescription: Get dressed without bending past a new hip or knee — sock aid, long shoehorn, and dressing stick.

children.text: After a hip or knee replacement, you're told not to bend past 90 degrees — which makes getting dressed surprisingly hard. This kit (sock aid, 24-inch shoehorn, button hook, and dressing stick) is the standard recommendation from occupational therapists.

name: Stander Bed Assist Rail
shortDescription: A handle that slides under the mattress and gives you something to grab getting in and out of bed.

children.text: Getting out of bed is a daily fall risk. A bed assist rail slides under the mattress and provides a sturdy handhold for the sit-to-stand, plus an organizer pouch for the phone and glasses. No tools, fits most home beds.

name: Gait Belt with Quick-Release Buckle (60")
shortDescription: Gives a caregiver a safe, firm hold during transfers and walking — protects both of you.

children.text: A gait belt is the single most important caregiver tool for safe transfers. It gives you a firm handhold around the person's waist so you're not pulling on an arm or under the shoulders. Quick-release buckle and a washable woven belt.

name: Weekly AM/PM Pill Organizer

children.text: Missed and doubled doses are a real hazard with a long medication list. A clear AM/PM organizer makes it obvious at a glance whether today's pills were taken. Large compartments and lids that open without a fight for arthritic hands.

name: Nitrile Exam Gloves (Box of 100)
shortDescription: Powder-free, latex-free exam gloves for dressing changes and personal care. Sold by the box.

children.text: The everyday glove for home caregiving — powder-free and latex-free so there's no allergy worry. Textured fingertips for grip during dressing changes and personal care. Tell us your size; we stock S through XL.

name: 3M N95 Respirator Masks (10-pack)
shortDescription: NIOSH-approved N95 respirators for higher-risk caregiving and immunocompromised households.

children.text: When a surgical mask isn't enough — caring for someone immunocompromised, or during respiratory illness season — an N95 filters at least 95% of airborne particles. NIOSH-approved, individually usable for several wears if undamaged.

name: Procedure Masks, Level 1 (Box of 50)
shortDescription: Everyday earloop masks for general caregiving and clinic visits. Box of 50.

children.text: The standard disposable earloop mask for routine caregiving, appointments, and waiting rooms. Three-ply with a soft inner layer and an adjustable nose wire. Box of 50 for households that go through them.

name: Disposable Isolation Gowns (10-pack)

children.text: For the messier parts of caregiving — wound irrigation, incontinence cleanup — a disposable gown keeps your clothes clean and reduces cross-contamination. Full-back coverage with tie closures, one size fits most.

title: Rollator vs. walker: which one's right for you?
excerpt: The honest difference, who should pick each, and the one question that settles it in thirty seconds.

children.text: We have this conversation five times a week: a customer comes in and points at the aisle full of rolling four-wheeled things and the aisle full of two-handled frames, and asks which they actually need. Let's make it simple.
The thirty-second answer
Do you need to
lean heavily on the device for stability
? If yes, you want a walker — the ones with two rubber-tipped feet and two wheels, or four feet. If you mostly need something to
hold onto for balance and give you somewhere to rest
, you want a rollator.
Walker — when it's the right pick

A walker requires you to partially lift it and place it forward with each step. That's a feature, not a bug: the lift-and-place motion is what anchors the device and gives you something solid to push against.
You've had significant leg weakness or balance loss
You need to lean noticeably forward during use
You're recovering from a major lower-body surgery
A physical therapist has specifically recommended a walker
Rollator — when it's the right pick
A rollator rolls on four wheels, so you walk through it rather than lifting it. Hand brakes let you control speed. Most have a seat for rest breaks. Most also have a basket for groceries.
You can maintain an upright posture
You walk longer distances (grocery store, neighborhood walks)
You need somewhere to rest mid-walk
You're more worried about fatigue than instability
The in-between case: a hybrid or upgraded walker

There's a middle category: a walker with front wheels and back glides. It gives you the anchoring motion of a walker but rolls slightly when you push. If you're genuinely torn, this is often the answer.
Come try both

We have the full floor of both. A five-minute walk-around on each tells you more than any article. Bring the shoes you'd actually wear, and — honestly — bring the stairs-to-door-to-car thought in your head so we can check the footprint.

seo.title: Rollator vs. walker — which do you actually need?
seo.description: A plain-English comparison from people who fit both every day. One question settles it in thirty seconds.

title: CPAP mask sizing: a complete guide
excerpt: Why mask fit matters more than the machine, how to size each of the three mask types, and the mistakes that cause most failures.

children.text: 90% of people who abandon CPAP therapy do so because of their mask, not the machine. If you're just starting out or you're on your second machine and still struggling, the problem is almost certainly mask fit. Here's how we approach it.
The three types
Nasal pillow
Smallest footprint. Two soft inserts sit just inside the nostrils. Best for people who breathe through their nose, side sleepers, and people who don't want anything across the face.
Nasal mask
Covers the nose only with a triangular cushion. The most common starting mask. Good for mild beards. You must breathe through your nose for it to work.
Full face mask

Covers both nose and mouth. Required for mouth-breathers, people with frequent nasal congestion, or those with high-pressure therapy needs. Larger, slightly louder, but the right choice for a third of users.
Why sizing matters

Too small and the cushion digs in and leaves marks. Too large and air escapes at the seal and the therapy stops working. 'Sort of fits' is not acceptable — mask fit needs to be correct or therapy fails.
Our fitting process
Ask about your sleep position, breathing pattern, and facial hair
Try two or three candidate masks from different sizes
Run the actual CPAP at your prescribed pressure with each mask
Lie down on our demo surface — mask behavior changes when horizontal
Confirm no leaks at the eyes, cheeks, or lips
The five most common mistakes we see
Tightening headgear straps to stop leaks — almost always the wrong answer. The wrong cushion size is.
Keeping a mask that leaves red marks 'because it works' — those marks mean it's too tight
Buying a cheap mask online before trying it in person
Not replacing cushions on schedule — they stretch and lose seal
Shaving to make a mask fit instead of choosing a mask that handles facial hair

seo.title: CPAP mask sizing — the complete guide
seo.description: Why fit matters more than the machine, how to choose between nasal pillow, nasal, and full-face masks.

title: Bath safety checklist for aging in place
excerpt: Print this, walk through the bathroom, and you'll prevent the majority of falls. Done in one afternoon.

children.text: The bathroom is the most dangerous room in the house for anyone over 65. Wet surfaces, low sit-to-stand heights, and narrow spaces combine to create falls — and bathroom falls cause more fractures than any other kind. Here's the checklist.
Bolt-in grab bars
Not suction mounts. Suction mounts are convenient; they are
not
load-bearing. Every grab bar that actually prevents falls is bolted into studs or with rated drywall anchors.
One grab bar beside the toilet, vertical or diagonal
One grab bar at the shower entrance, vertical
One grab bar on the shower wall, horizontal at shoulder height for seated use
Raised toilet seat with arms
Adds 3–4 inches to the toilet height, making the sit-stand motion much gentler on knees and hips. The arms give push-off leverage.
Shower chair or transfer bench
Choose a transfer bench if the bathroom has a tub — it straddles the tub wall so you don't step over. Choose a shower chair if it's a walk-in shower.
Non-slip surfaces
Non-slip mat inside the tub/shower
Non-slip bath mat outside the shower
Remove any throw rugs or secure them with non-slip backing
Good lighting
Bathrooms often have one overhead bulb. Add a night light that comes on automatically and a task light at the sink. Falls at night, on the way to the bathroom, are the most common.
The afternoon plan
One Saturday afternoon. Two people. A trip to us to pick up the equipment, an hour to install the grab bars (we'll loan you the stud finder), and a quick swap of mats. Done.

seo.title: Bath safety checklist for aging in place
seo.description: Print-and-walk checklist. The four items that prevent most bathroom falls.

title: What to buy (and rent) before knee surgery
excerpt: Your surgeon handed you a list. Here's what actually matters, what's worth renting, and what you can skip.

children.text: After a total knee replacement, most people are non-weight-bearing or partial-weight-bearing for 2–6 weeks and need mobility aids for 8–12 weeks. Here's our ortho-patient shopping list, honest.
Rent, don't buy
Knee scooter or walker
— you'll use it for 4–8 weeks, then never again
Elevated toilet seat
— useful for 4–6 weeks
Ice machine
(cold therapy unit) — typically 1–2 weeks
Buy — you'll use it forever
Grabber/reacher
— cheap, useful indefinitely
Sock aid and long-handled shoehorn
— you can't bend to put socks on for weeks
Compression stockings
— per surgeon's prescription
Buy only if you don't already own
Shower chair or transfer bench
Grab bars (install before surgery)
Wedge pillow for leg elevation
Timing
Pick up equipment 5–7 days before surgery, not the day of. You'll want to practice using the knee scooter or walker before you're in post-op fog.
Drop by the store and we'll walk through your surgeon's list item by item.

title: FSA/HSA: what medical equipment is actually covered?
excerpt: The short list of yes, maybe, and no — based on IRS Publication 502, not marketing copy.

children.text: Most DME is FSA/HSA eligible. Some of it requires a Letter of Medical Necessity (LMN). A few items are tricky. Here's our plain-language guide — confirm anything uncertain with your plan administrator.
Definitely eligible (no LMN needed)
CPAP machines, masks, tubing, filters, chamber cleaners
Mobility aids: canes, walkers, rollators, wheelchairs, crutches
Bath safety: grab bars, shower chairs, transfer benches, raised toilet seats
Compression stockings (15 mmHg and above)
Wound care: gauze, bandages, antiseptic
Incontinence products
Blood pressure monitors, glucose meters, thermometers
Eligible with a Letter of Medical Necessity
Air purifiers (if prescribed for allergy/asthma)
Orthopedic shoes (partial — only the specific medical portion)
Some home modifications (grab bar
installation
may be eligible even when the bar is)
Not eligible
Weight-loss products (unless LMN)
Vitamins and supplements (with some narrow exceptions)
Cosmetic items
Hygiene products not specifically medical
Using your card at our store

We accept FSA/HSA debit cards at the register. If your card is declined, it's usually because the merchant isn't coded as DME in the IRS IIAS system — call your plan administrator and we can provide an itemized receipt for reimbursement.

title: How to sanitize a CPAP machine (and what to skip)
excerpt: Warm water, mild soap, air dry. Why the fancy ozone cleaners aren't worth it.

children.text: CPAP hygiene matters: you're inhaling through the mask and tubing for 8 hours a night. But it's also simpler than the marketing around ozone cleaners would have you believe. Here's the protocol.
Daily
Wipe the mask cushion with a damp cloth
Empty the humidifier water chamber and refill with distilled water
Weekly
Hand-wash the mask cushion and frame with warm water and mild dish soap, rinse thoroughly, air dry
Hand-wash the tubing the same way
Wipe the outside of the machine with a dry cloth
Monthly
Fully wash the humidifier water chamber, let air dry completely before reassembling
Check and replace the air filter (white disposable filter inside the back of the machine)
What to skip

Ozone and UV cleaning devices (SoClean and similar) are marketed aggressively but not FDA-approved for CPAP cleaning, and ResMed specifically voids warranty on machines that show signs of ozone residue. Stick with the warm-water-and-soap method — it works and costs $0.

seo.title: How to clean your CPAP — the simple, FDA-aligned protocol
seo.description: Daily, weekly, monthly. Skip the ozone cleaners.

title: Transferring a loved one from wheelchair to bed safely
excerpt: The body mechanics, the equipment that helps, and the injuries to avoid — for caregivers doing this alone.

children.text: If you're transferring someone from a wheelchair to a bed or vice versa multiple times a day, your back is at risk. Here's how to do it without injury — to you or the person you're caring for.
The equipment that helps
Gait belt
— a wide belt around the waist you can grip for pulling leverage
Transfer board
— a slick board that bridges the wheelchair and bed so you slide instead of lift
Hoyer lift
— powered lift for anyone who can't bear weight at all; required if the person is 160+ lb and you're alone
The sit-to-stand transfer (person can bear some weight)
Lock the wheelchair brakes
Put a gait belt on the person
Put your foot against the outside of their foot to block their knee from collapsing
Count to three together — on three, they push off the chair arms while you pull up on the belt
Pivot ninety degrees; sit them on the bed
Your body
Feet shoulder-width apart, knees bent, back straight — lift with the legs
Never twist while lifting; pivot with your feet
If you feel back strain more than once, you need a mechanical lift — this is not sustainable

seo.title: Transferring a loved one from wheelchair to bed safely
seo.description: Body mechanics, equipment, and injury avoidance for at-home caregivers.

title: Choosing compression stockings: mmHg explained
excerpt: A plain-English chart from 8-15 mmHg casual support through 40 mmHg therapeutic.

children.text: Compression stocking packaging is covered in numbers that only make sense if you already know what they mean. Here's the translation.
The ranges
8–15 mmHg (mild):
Everyday support. For travel, long standing days, mild leg fatigue. No prescription needed.
15–20 mmHg (firm):
Mild medical compression. Tired, achy legs, mild swelling, early varicose veins. Most common OTC medical compression.
20–30 mmHg (class 1 medical):
Standard post-surgical and chronic venous insufficiency compression. Typically prescribed.
30–40 mmHg (class 2 medical):
Heavy therapeutic compression. Severe venous disease, active ulcers. Always prescribed.
40+ mmHg (class 3 medical):
Specialist-prescribed only. Rare.
Wrong level = wrong effect
Going
too high
in mmHg can actually reduce circulation in someone with arterial disease. That's why high-mmHg compression requires a prescription and a check that you don't have peripheral arterial disease first.
Sizing
Always measured in the morning before swelling starts. Ankle circumference, calf circumference, and — for thigh-high styles — thigh circumference. We measure in store.

seo.title: Compression stockings explained — mmHg by level
seo.description: From 8-15 mmHg casual to 30-40 mmHg therapeutic. Plain English.

title: Knee scooter or crutches after foot surgery?
excerpt: The question every foot-surgery patient asks. Here's our honest answer.

children.text: For non-weight-bearing foot surgery (Achilles, bunionectomy, metatarsal surgery, calcaneal fractures), you have two options for getting around: crutches or a knee scooter. We rent out roughly fifty knee scooters a month and a handful of crutches. There's a reason.
Knee scooter wins for most people
No upper-body strength required — your knee rests on the padded platform
No armpit or hand soreness like crutches cause
Faster and more stable over distance
Can carry a basket or cup holder
Crutches win if...
You need to navigate many stairs
Your home has narrow hallways (under 32 inches) that a scooter can't fit through
You only need them for a short period (under a week) — easier to just grab and go
The hybrid plan
Many patients rent a knee scooter for daily use and keep a set of crutches on hand for stairs. We rent knee scooters for $35/week with a $50 deposit; crutches are $15/week.

title: Hospital bed at home: what to expect
excerpt: Setup, placement, rental timeline, and the questions families ask before they bring one home.

children.text: Bringing a hospital bed home is a decision families often resist even when it would obviously help. Here's what to actually expect.
Placement
Plan for 3–4 feet of clearance on one side for caregiver access
Near an outlet — the bed plugs in
Near the bathroom if mobility is limited
On the main floor — avoid requiring stairs
Setup

We deliver and set up. Takes 20 minutes. Mattress is included (a basic foam; we can swap for pressure-relief if pressure sores are a concern). We walk you through the remote — head, foot, and overall height functions.
Typical rental periods
Post-op recovery:
4–8 weeks
Hospice:
until no longer needed
Chronic care:
long-term — we sell used beds for long-term use at 40–60% of new
The conversation families have
Hospital beds at home often feel like a defeat. Reframe: it's the piece of equipment that lets someone stay
in their own home
instead of moving to a facility. Most families, in hindsight, wish they'd brought one in earlier.

title: Starting with incontinence products: a practical guide
excerpt: Absorbency, fit, skin care. What to buy first and how to avoid waste.

children.text: The first few weeks of managing incontinence (yours or a loved one's) involves a lot of over-buying, under-buying, and trial-and-error. Here's how to skip most of it.
Start with the right absorbency
Light pads (150–400ml) are often enough for occasional stress incontinence. Overnight briefs (1500ml+) are what you need if you're managing overnight heavy incontinence. Don't buy one size fits all.
Buy a variety pack first
Skin sensitivities, fit preferences, and absorbency needs are personal. Buy two or three small packs of different brands before committing to a case. We'll help you pick the trial mix.
Non-negotiable: skin care
Zinc-based barrier cream at every change
Gentle cleanser (no fragrance, no alcohol)
Change products as soon as practical — not later
FSA/HSA
Most incontinence products are FSA/HSA eligible. Use the card at our register.

title: Traveling with a CPAP: practical tips
excerpt: Airline rules, battery options, water hacks, and when to use the AirMini.

children.text: Traveling with a CPAP is simpler than people think. Here's the practical guide — airlines, destinations, and the AirMini argument.
Airlines
CPAP machines are medical devices. They travel in your carry-on and
don't count toward your carry-on limit
. Tell TSA it's a CPAP during screening; it'll go through as a medical device.
Water
Use distilled water in the humidifier where possible. At a hotel, bottled water is fine for one or two nights; don't use tap for longer — scale buildup damages the machine.
Power
Most machines auto-detect 100–240V globally. Bring a plug adapter, not a voltage converter. For camping or flights over 6 hours, ResMed's Power Station II gives you about a night of therapy.
Use the AirMini for extended travel
If you travel more than 3–4 times a year, the AirMini pays for itself in suitcase space and setup time.

seo.title: Traveling with a CPAP — practical tips
seo.description: Airline rules, water hacks, power options, and the AirMini argument.

title: Grab bar placement: where they actually belong
excerpt: Three locations that prevent most bathroom falls — and one place people install them by mistake.

children.text: Where you place a grab bar matters more than how many you install. Here's the short version.
The three high-impact spots
Vertical bar at the shower entrance (for the step-in transition)
Horizontal bar inside the shower at shoulder height (for seated use)
Vertical or diagonal bar beside the toilet (for sit-to-stand)
The mistake

Installing a horizontal bar across the back wall of the tub. It looks helpful. It's not well-positioned for how people actually fall — forward or sideways as they step into the tub. Vertical at the entrance beats horizontal across the back, every time.

seo.title: Grab bar placement that actually prevents falls
seo.description: Three spots, one common mistake.

title: Choosing a cane: single-point vs. quad
excerpt: Single-point for mild balance help, quad for real stability. Here's how to choose.

children.text: Canes come in two families. Single-point canes give you mild balance support and add reach. Quad canes (four tips at the base) give you meaningfully more stability and stand up by themselves.
Single-point
Right if you mostly want something to touch the ground for balance reassurance. Comes in adjustable-height and fixed-height. Foldable options exist for travel.
Quad cane

Right if you actually need the cane to bear weight. The four-point base gives you a stable platform. Slightly heavier and slower. Small-base quad for indoors; large-base quad for more support outdoors.
How to size
Standing upright with arms at your side, the cane should come up to the crease of your wrist. Slightly shorter is okay; too tall strains the shoulder.

title: Winter mobility: ice cleats, cane tips, and when to stay home
excerpt: Portland ice days are rare but real. Here's how to handle them.

children.text: Portland gets a handful of ice days a year. For anyone using a rollator or cane, here's what to do.
Ice cleats that slip over your shoes — we stock a few styles
Swap your standard cane tip for a retractable ice-point tip
Rollators with 6-inch wheels handle light slush; larger 8-inch wheels handle packed snow
When in doubt — stay in. One icy walk isn't worth a broken hip.

title: Reading a DME insurance denial letter
excerpt: What the denial codes mean and how to appeal in plain English.

children.text: Insurance denials on DME claims are usually fixable. Most denials fall into three patterns:
Missing documentation
— usually the Letter of Medical Necessity. Ask your provider to resubmit.
Wrong HCPCS code
— the biller used the wrong code for the equipment. Call the supplier to recode.
Non-covered item
— the plan genuinely doesn't cover this equipment. Options: appeal with additional justification, pay out-of-pocket, or use FSA/HSA.
We don't bill insurance for retail — you'd pay us and seek reimbursement. We'll provide a detailed itemized receipt with the right HCPCS codes on request.

title: Preventing pressure sores at home
excerpt: Repositioning schedules, mattress options, and the early signs to catch.

children.text: For anyone spending most of their day in a bed or wheelchair, pressure sores are a real risk. Prevention is dramatically easier than treatment.
Reposition on a schedule
Bed-bound: every 2 hours
Wheelchair-bound: every 30 minutes when able
Mattress upgrades
For anyone bed-bound more than 4–6 weeks, swap the basic mattress for a pressure-relief foam or alternating-pressure air mattress. We rent both.
Early signs to catch
Red or darker skin over a bony area that doesn't fade within 30 minutes of removing pressure
Skin that feels warmer or cooler than surrounding areas
Any skin breakdown — clinician immediately

title: How to size a walker or rollator correctly
excerpt: The elbow-bend rule and why it matters for your shoulders.

children.text: A walker or rollator set to the wrong height doesn't just feel off — it causes shoulder and back pain over weeks of use.
The rule
Stand upright with arms relaxed at your sides. The handles of the walker should come up to your wrist crease. When you grip the handles, your elbows should bend at about 15 degrees.
Signs it's wrong
Shoulders hunching upward — handles too high
Leaning forward at the hips to reach — handles too low
Lower back pain after using — almost always height

title: Oxygen at home: concentrator or tank?
excerpt: The decision we walk through with every new oxygen patient.

children.text: Note: home oxygen is highly regulated and we coordinate with your provider rather than selling directly. But here's the framework.
Concentrator (stationary)
Plugs into the wall, produces oxygen from room air. Ideal for home use. Some patients never need anything else.
Portable oxygen concentrator (POC)
Battery-powered, airline-approved. Right if you travel or leave home for hours at a stretch. More expensive; usually coordinated through insurance.
Oxygen tanks
Filled with compressed oxygen. Portable, silent, no batteries. Heavier than a POC. Used as backup or for patients with specific oxygen flow needs a concentrator can't match.
Call us with your prescription and flow-rate requirements; we'll connect you with a local oxygen provider we trust.

title: Aging in place: a 30-minute planning checklist
excerpt: Walk through your parent's home with this checklist. It changes the conversation.

children.text: Adult children often know something needs to change but don't know where to start. This is a concrete 30-minute walkthrough.
Bedroom
Bed handle or rail
Nightlight to the bathroom
Phone within reach from bed
Bathroom
Bolted grab bars (shower entrance, shower wall, toilet)
Raised toilet seat with arms
Shower chair or transfer bench
Non-slip mats inside and outside tub
Kitchen
Remove throw rugs
Move frequently-used items to waist-to-shoulder height
Reacher/grabber for higher items
Living areas
Clear pathways of cords and clutter
Firm chair with arms near the TV
Stairs: handrails on both sides, non-slip treads
Technology
Medical alert pendant
Easy-to-dial phone
Medication reminder system
Bring this list in, and we'll go through it item by item.
